summary: Simon Späti documents his migration from WordPress to Hugo, a static site generator. After years of struggling with WordPress plugin management, template incompatibilities, and the friction of converting markdown content to WordPress format, he found Hugo offered a simpler, git-based workflow. The migration was straightforward using an export plugin, and while page speed improvements were modest, the real value was in treating content as code—editable locally, version-controlled, and deployable with simple scripts. He describes the move as a 'homecoming' to the simplicity of his early web development days.
key_points: WordPress plugin management became unsustainable—constant updates, compatibility issues with Gutenberg, and production-only editing created ongoing maintenance burden
Static site generators like Hugo enable a code-like workflow: markdown files, git versioning, local preview, and simple deployment
Migration from WordPress to Hugo was surprisingly easy using existing export tools that converted posts to markdown with images intact
Page speed improved but wasn't the main benefit—the real win was workflow simplicity and having content accessible as files rather than locked in a database
The experience rekindled his enthusiasm for writing by removing friction from the publishing process
money_quotes: I always fancied something that works well with git and treats the text as code.

The real power for me, at least, is having the text right in front of me as markdown same as code, easily pushable to git. Compared to before, where it was hidden away in a MySQL database with no real access.
It surprised me how much fun it can be to write and generate new content again! I guess only for that fact I will create more content in the future than I did lately.
I feel like going back to where it started in 2005, where everything is small and simple in one (git) repo.
The best part is that security, scale and performance are improved radically with static site generation.
key_insight: Treating blog content as code—version-controlled markdown files with local preview—removes enough friction that it actually makes writing fun again.
